rationales.power: When Khalid took over his update and "quoted numbers I had gathered," he was left feeling invisible instead of able to claim authority over his own work.
rationales.conformity: He "just sat there" because interrupting might have "looked aggressive," showing restraint to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.power: The request is stalled because Hassan is "reviewing priorities," leaving him trapped while "someone else holds the cards."
rationales.benevolence: He keeps pushing for the portable ultrasound because "we need that unit for the patients on my ward," centering their wellbeing.

rationales.achievement: He quickly reorganized coverage when two nurses called in sick and takes pride that "the ward ran well today" under pressure.
rationales.power: He gathered everyone, "laid out what we had, and asked who could do what," effectively directing the team through the crisis.
rationales.benevolence: He focused on solving the staffing gap "together" and keeping the ward steady rather than forcing people, supporting coworkers and patients.
He gave Amira full responsibility, then told her in front of the team that her report was "solid work," offering trust and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: He challenged the regional template because it "doesn't account for the patient demographics we see," defending his own judgment over a rigid standard.
rationales.achievement: He had already drafted his own protocol from "patterns I've been tracking on my ward for months," showing sustained effort to improve performance.

rationales.power: He immediately corrected being introduced as "one of the senior nurses" because the title and authority of ward lead clearly matter to him.
Dr. Rashid "steamrolled right over me" and he felt "made invisible," highlighting a loss of influence in the room.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because he fears being seen as "the angry nurse" and accepts that "if a doctor talks over you, that's just how it is."

rationales.achievement: He pursued the nursing leadership conference, arranged his own coverage, and is frustrated that the sign-off is blocking a concrete professional development step.
He spent "two evenings to prepare" a documented staffing case with patient and incident data, showing strong investment in competent performance.
rationales.power: He concludes the real decisions happen through "relationships, politics" and conversations he is excluded from, leaving him without influence.

rationales.achievement: He keeps "building the case" and says "I don't think I can stop preparing," showing a persistent drive to perform well even when recognition fails.

rationales.power: She defends taking charge because "if I do not, things fall apart," showing a strong need to direct how the committee runs.
rationales.conformity: She "stayed quiet" because defending herself would make her look exactly like what she was accused of, so she restrained herself to avoid social backlash.

rationales.benevolence: She brought "three containers of biryani" to an exhausted family and says "showing up when people need you" is what matters.
She spent "an hour consoling" Priya after the exclusion at school, focusing her energy on her daughter's distress.

rationales.self_direction: She says she wanted to ask to check with Ravi first, but instead "just absorbed it like always," suppressing her own choice.
rationales.tradition: She immediately agreed to host Ravi's parents because family duty felt non-negotiable, despite the disruption it would cause.
rationales.benevolence: She says yes to caring for her in-laws for six weeks and starts reorganizing meals and space around their needs.

rationales.achievement: She lists the complex work of managing finances, schedules, festivals, and school needs, insisting it is real labor rather than "gave up" ambition.
rationales.benevolence: Her examples center on supporting close family members, from proofreading applications to sewing Priya's recital costume at midnight.

rationales.power: She "volunteered to step back from leading the Navratri planning," intentionally giving up the role directing vendors and volunteers.

rationales.hedonism: She savored "chai" and fifteen quiet minutes on the balcony, explicitly noticing the simple pleasure of doing nothing.

rationales.benevolence: She is proud that Vikram is "kind" and that family members "showed up," emphasizing care for people close to him.
rationales.universalism: She highlights his food drive for "international students struggling during spring break," approving concern for people beyond the immediate family.

rationales.power: She says the kitchen is "the one domain where I should have some say," yet even there she feels forced to "just smile and adjust."
rationales.conformity: She "did not say anything" and keeps accommodating the situation to avoid open conflict with her guest.
rationales.tradition: She defers to her elder mother-in-law as "seventy-two and... a guest," accepting the role expectations despite resentment.
rationales.benevolence: She "made everyone's favorite dinner anyway," continuing to care for the household even while upset.

rationales.power: She insists that "someone has to keep track of things" and keeps reminding Priya because she feels responsible for directing how things run.
rationales.security: She imagines that without her intervention there would be "chaos," showing a strong pull toward order and preventing instability.
rationales.benevolence: Her controlling reminders are framed as care for Priya's wellbeing and future, not indifference: "If I do not push her, she will coast."

rationales.self_direction: She finally told Amma "this is my house and my family," asserting her own judgment after weeks of correction.
rationales.benevolence: In the response she says she wants to be remembered for "showed up" and feeding people because "you cannot love someone properly if you let them go hungry."
